courseShortDescription: Test course for workplace rights and Fair Work system.
courseDescription: A test course containing a single lesson on work, wellbeing, workplace participants and the Australian Fair Work system.

slides.content:
LESSON 1 — Work and Wellbeing + Workplace Participants

Lesson Focus
How workplaces function through interaction between employees, employers, government and unions, and how the Fair Work system regulates fairness.

Work and Wellbeing
Work provides more than income. It contributes to financial security, independence, purpose, social connection and long-term stability. A well-regulated workplace therefore improves both economic wellbeing (ability to support oneself) and social wellbeing (health, security and life satisfaction).
Because work affects quality of life, governments regulate employment relationships to ensure workers are treated fairly and safely.

Workplace Participants
Workplaces operate through interaction between several groups. Each group has different responsibilities and interests, and the law exists to balance them.

Employees
An employee is a person who performs work for another individual or organisation in return for payment.
Employment may vary in:
- hours worked
- level of responsibility
- type of tasks performed
Employees are entitled to safe working conditions, fair pay and legal protections. They must also follow workplace rules and lawful instructions.

Employers
An employer is a person or business that hires workers.
Employers have legal responsibilities, including:
- providing a safe workplace
- paying correct wages
- providing training and supervision
- contributing to superannuation
- respecting employee rights and working conditions
Employers benefit from labour, but in exchange must meet legal standards designed to protect workers.

Governments
Governments regulate workplaces to ensure fairness and stability across the economy.
They establish rules covering:
- minimum wages
- workplace safety
- leave entitlements
- working conditions
Without regulation, employers could reduce wages or conditions to gain competitive advantage, creating unsafe and unfair workplaces. Therefore government intervention maintains minimum standards for all workers.

Trade Unions
Trade unions are organisations representing workers’ interests.
Their main goals are:
- higher wages
- safer working conditions
- job security
Membership is voluntary, but unions collectively negotiate with employers to improve conditions.
The peak union body in Australia is the Australian Council of Trade Unions (ACTU), which represents many affiliated unions and has historically influenced major worker protections such as compulsory superannuation.

The Fair Work System
Australia regulates employment primarily through the Fair Work Act 2009.
This law establishes minimum workplace standards and governs the relationship between employers and employees.
It provides a national safety net consisting of:
- National Employment Standards (NES)
- Modern Awards
- National Minimum Wage

National Employment Standards (NES)
The NES are minimum legal entitlements applying to most employees.
They include:
- Maximum 38 ordinary weekly hours
- Flexible work arrangements for carers/parents
- Casual conversion rights after 12 months
- Parental leave (up to 12 months unpaid + extension request)
- 4 weeks annual leave
- Personal/carer’s leave and compassionate leave
- Community service leave (including jury duty)
- Long service leave
- Public holiday entitlements
- Notice of termination and redundancy pay
- Fair Work Information Statement for new employees
These standards cannot be removed by contracts or agreements.

Fair Work Commission (FWC)
The Fair Work Commission is Australia’s national workplace tribunal.
It acts as an independent umpire and can:
- hear workplace disputes
- rule on unfair dismissal claims
- approve enterprise agreements
- set minimum wages
- adjust modern awards
Its decisions are legally binding.

Fair Work Ombudsman (FWO)
The Fair Work Ombudsman ensures compliance with workplace laws.
It:
- investigates breaches
- resolves pay disputes
- educates workers and employers
- enforces workplace entitlements
Employees can contact the FWO if they believe they are underpaid or treated unlawfully.

Key Idea
The workplace operates as a regulated relationship:
- employees provide labour
- employers provide wages
- unions advocate for workers
- government sets minimum standards
The Fair Work system exists to balance power and prevent exploitation while maintaining productive businesses.

Video (Overview of workplace rights & Fair Work system)
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=57O0rb9OjDQ

quiz.question: Explain how the Fair Work system protects employees in Australian workplaces.
options: It sets enforceable minimum standards and provides independent bodies to resolve disputes and enforce workplace laws.
It tells employees which jobs to choose and guarantees promotions.
It replaces employers with government officials in every workplace.
It only regulates tax and superannuation, not working conditions.
quiz.explanation: The Fair Work Act 2009 sets minimum conditions through the National Employment Standards. The Fair Work Commission resolves disputes and unfair dismissal claims, while the Fair Work Ombudsman investigates breaches and educates workers about pay and entitlements.

quiz.question: An employee is best defined as:
options: A person who owns a business
A person who works for payment under an employer
quiz.explanation: An employee works for another person or organisation in return for payment.

quiz.question: Which group sets minimum workplace standards in Australia?
quiz.explanation: Governments create laws, including the Fair Work Act, that set minimum standards.

quiz.question: The Fair Work Act 2009 mainly regulates:
quiz.explanation: The Fair Work Act governs the relationship between employers and employees.

quiz.question: Which organisation acts as the workplace tribunal?
quiz.explanation: The Fair Work Commission is Australia’s national workplace tribunal.

quiz.question: The Fair Work Ombudsman primarily:
quiz.explanation: The FWO investigates breaches, resolves pay disputes and enforces workplace entitlements.

quiz.question: Which of the following is an employer responsibility?
quiz.explanation: Employers must provide a safe workplace and meet legal standards.

quiz.question: Trade unions mainly aim to:
quiz.explanation: Unions negotiate for higher wages, safer conditions and job security on behalf of workers.

quiz.question: National Employment Standards (NES) apply to:
options: All employees covered by the Fair Work system
quiz.explanation: NES are minimum legal entitlements for most employees in the Fair Work system.

quiz.question: Maximum ordinary weekly working hours under NES are:
quiz.explanation: NES set a maximum of 38 ordinary weekly hours for most employees.

quiz.question: Which organisation approves enterprise agreements?
quiz.explanation: The Fair Work Commission approves enterprise agreements and ensures they meet legal standards.
